title: Carnot Cycle P-V Indicator Diagram
subject: Thermodynamics
textbook_reference: R.K. Rajput, Thermal Engineering, Laxmi Publications, Chapter 5, Page 190

recent_questions: State and prove Carnot's Theorem. Explain the four processes of a Carnot cycle with a P-V diagram (Dec 2023).
Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot engine (May 2023).

theory: The Carnot Cycle is a theoretical thermodynamic cycle proposed by Nicolas Léonard Sadi Carnot in 1824. It represents the limit of thermal efficiency for a heat engine operating between two temperatures (T_hot and T_cold). It is composed of four reversible processes: (1) Reversible Isothermal Expansion (1➔2) at T_hot, where heat Q_in is absorbed; (2) Reversible Adiabatic Expansion (2➔3), where temperature drops from T_hot to T_cold; (3) Reversible Isothermal Compression (3➔4) at T_cold, where heat Q_out is rejected; and (4) Reversible Adiabatic Compression (4➔1), restoring the gas to its initial state. The Carnot efficiency depends solely on the operating temperatures: n = 1 - T_cold / T_hot.
key_points: Process 1➔2: Isothermal expansion (constant temperature, volume increases).
Process 2➔3: Adiabatic expansion (no heat transfer, temperature drops).
Process 3➔4: Isothermal compression (constant temperature, volume decreases).
Process 4➔1: Adiabatic compression (no heat transfer, temperature rises).
No real engine can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same two reservoirs.
use_cases: Establishing the maximum theoretical limit of power plant efficiency.
Thermodynamic modeling of advanced refrigeration and heat pump cycles.
